speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother during the war in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add about your father and his time interned on the Isle of Man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the Isle of Man, from what he told me, he, he, he was actually the postman there. And they all had to find some sort of an occupation. And he, he in fact, dished out the post, I think. He had, I mean, he wasn't imprisoned there. Though naturally, he was away from his wife. But by and large, he had a very comfortable life like everybody else did. And very soon afterwards-- and I really don't know to what extent, to what the length of time he stayed there-- he was released. And he then went back to the home where-- where he lived with his wife. On his birthday in the air raid shelter, when I was in the air raid shelters in the evenings at night, I had very little else to do, and so I took up knitting. And I-- wool was, of course, rationed during the war, but I obtained somehow some eight ounces of gray wool. And I started knitting a scarf for him. And the stitches, although the pattern said 60 stitches, it varied from 55 to 65, I very proudly sent that to my father in London. I got a thank you. They acknowledged the parcel, but a week later I got the parcel back from my mother which contained two pairs of gray socks. And she said, your father unfortunately doesn't like wearing a scarf, and so [LAUGHS] I've undone the scarf, and I've made some socks out of it for you. My parents were bombed out three or four times by doodlebugs and various other devices. And they lived in the underground, South, South Wimbledon Underground Station for about three months. They lived in a hospital, in the basement of a hospital and so on, together with hundreds of other people. They had a very uncomfortable life during the war. But they tried to stick to London as much as they could. When I finished my school at the age of 14, I had to look for a job. And I was very, very fortunate to be offered an apprenticeship as a tool maker in a tool making company in-- in Walsall in Staffordshire. And I started work there-- we worked there, excuse me, we worked there for the 12-hour-- 12-hour shifts, two weeks on days and two weeks on nights. And I think I gained a-- a tremendous experience which later on in my career development as an engineer stood me in very, very good standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was still war work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, very much. This was-- this started in 1942. And at that time, we were granted-- each war worker was granted-- one week's holiday. And I was always very interested in plants and agriculture and natural things. And so a friend of mine, also a refugee from Breslau who served an apprenticeship as a-- as an industrial chemist in Birmingham, we decided to go to a camp. We decided to go and dig for victory, and the easiest way of doing that was to attach oneself to a youth camp of which there were quite a lot of those in those days. I think it's appropriate to say here that for about a year I'd been toying with the idea of what, in fact, did Judaism offer me. We'd suffered tremendously. The family was torn to bits and pieces. OK, the four of us were still together in the UK in relative safety. But the rest of the family all perished or were suffering from tremendous atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't really see much point in maintaining this adherence. And-- to Judaism, who knows what was the future going to bring? And while I didn't become an agnostic, I didn't have much opportunity in Walsall anyway to retain Judaism per se. And so I let that whole concept of Judaism slip consciously until we went to this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp happened to have been run by an organization, the halutz organization, called Habonim. And it was at that camp, at which we were only for one week, we met people of our age group, boys and girls. Apart from the fact that I fell in love with one of the girls there, the boys and all the other people impressed me immensely. They seemed to have a specific purpose. They were Zionistically oriented. They wanted to live on the kibbutz, and they wanted to deal with agriculture. And I became smitten, if I can call it that, by the bug. The Habonim was organized extremely well throughout the country, and wherever there were Jewish communities, there were also Habonim groups. And so I was invited to the Habonim group in London, and there were about 18 of them, senior groups, two-- two Habonim groups in Birmingham. It flattered my ego. But at the same time, I was beginning to see some sort of contact with other people, many of whom had a similar sort of background to my own. There were, perhaps, in this Habonim movement, 50% of the members were refugees. The others were British born and obviously felt the pain of the people in a similar way as we did, perhaps not as directly as we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it gave me an association, and I became very, very keenly interested in this. And when I finally finished my apprenticeship after the war and moved to London to take on a job as a junior draftsman in a design-- in an engineering design office, I was approached by the Habonim people. Would I, perhaps, start a movement or a group of-- of similarly minded people in South London?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population in South London was rather scattered over a very wide area. It was not as concentrated as it was in many other areas such as Golders Green, perhaps, or Hampstead or perhaps even Edgware-Stanmore as it is today. And so I set that task, and within a year we had a number of groups of various age groups going there. And that in itself was a wonderful challenge, a terrific experience. And it was through the youth movement that I was called upon to fulfill a very specific task for the sake of Palestine, as it was then called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we come to that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before we say goodbye to the Midlands altogether, you just might like to say something about your military activities there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, my paramilitary duties-- they were rather odd. While I was apprenticed and-- I mentioned to you I was always interested in a certain amount of this military discipline and so on. And there was a war going on. I joined the Air Training Corps. This was an organization to train the young men between the ages of 16 and 18 in some pre-service entry activities. The idea was to join the Royal Air Force, and I was fortunate enough to be classified or accepted into the group that, in fact, was destined for air crew people. Flying seemed to be something that fascinated me. And so I was very happy there. And after two years, believe it or not, they went through the various records, personal records of people as a routine activity, and found that my nationality was German. And that was, of course, impossible as far as they were concerned because they considered me to be a hell of a security risk after I'd been with them for two years. And what sort of security problems one could arrange as a cadet who meets various evenings during the week and the weekends, I fail to see. But nevertheless, the law is the law. And so, regretfully, they had to dismiss me. At that time I was studying at evening classes in order to gain my matric so as to ultimately gain entry into university. And my English teacher, who oddly enough was a Scotsman, heard about this over a cup of tea. I told him I was depressed. I was expelled or released or dismissed. I was dismissed from the Air Training Corps. And he said, oh, man, you want to come and join our mob, the Home Guard. He was a captain in the Home Guard. And that, of course, I accepted with open arms, and so I joined the Home Guard. The irony of it all was that he introduced me to the commander of the local Home Guard battalion in Walsall. It was about 2,000 men strong and had, in fact, a number of anti-aircraft batteries that were manned partially by regular forces, mainly women, and partially by Home Guard members, who were part-time soldiers. They had a proper job during the day like I did, for instance, as a tool maker or apprentice tool maker, and weekends and free time, we were soldiers in the defense of the realm. The danger of a German invasion was always still with us. And so when I met this commanding officer, a colonel who'd been a colonel in his own right in the first World War and was too old now to serve in this current war, a very charming fellow, he told me to jump into the car. And he drove me to one or two of these anti-aircraft batteries. And he showed me how they operated. He showed me the control mechanisms, the control room, as it were. He showed me where the ammunition was being kept. And on the way back, he said, young man, I hope you appreciate that you and I are the only two people who know about the exact disposition of our armaments. And I thought what an ridiculous irony that on the one hand, I was being kicked out of the Air Training Corps because I was a security risk, and here, albeit it was only the Home Guard, nevertheless [LAUGHS] he opened up his entire secrets if we can call it that to a guy who was such a security risk. Anyway, I had a very interesting time in the Home Guard. I was seconded to the battalion intelligence section, not because I was intelligent but because I spoke German as well. And that was considered to be useful. In case we could take German prisoners, I'd be able to interrogate them and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- of course, the Home Guard was stood down long before the end of the war. I've got a really nice illuminated, sort of, document signed by HM the King thanking me, like everybody else, for their valiant services in the defense of the realm. It's an illuminated thing. It's quite nice to look at.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when in London, I, I, I became very active in the Habonim movement. And ultimately, that led me to be asked whether I would be prepared to undertake something a little bit more serious and strenuous than just running a, a, a, a youth movement with a Zionist ideology and then socialist views. And I said, yes, certainly. Anything that could be of value would be accepted. And so ultimately I was drafted into an organization that was possibly called Haganah in Europe-- I don't know what the official title of it was-- an organization that dealt fundamentally with illegal immigration into Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now in 1946, summer of 1946. And so I was sworn into this organization, absolutely forced to total and complete secrecy and everything else. And I heard nothing more about the whole thing for about nine months until one day I was being advised that I have to hold myself ready to undertake some strenuous duties, I think they called it. This might take anything up to six months, and it certainly wouldn't be in London. And I'd have to leave everything behind. By everything, I meant my job as a junior draftsman, my activities in the youth movement, and certainly my part-time studies at the university, because I was trying to gain qualifications as an engineer, having had some very good practical experience as an apprentice through my career.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I mentioned this to my parents, that I would not be available for some period of time, my father almost fell off his chair. He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know. How can you throw overboard your social ties, your family-- meaning himself and my mother-- and particularly your studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, young people have a [? throng, ?] a drive. They always know what is best, and I remained determined. In any case, there was no way of getting out of it because I'd already undertaken to serve the cause, whatever that cause was, which was totally unknown to me at the time. My mother had a little twinkle in her eye. She was rather more adventurous than my father. My father was very conservative with a small C and always thought you do the right thing at the right time. For instance, to illustrate this, he would never come to a table at, at, at lunchtime without wearing a tie, no matter how hot the weather may have been. He may have perhaps taken his tie off during the morning. But for lunch-- just en famille, just he and his wife and perhaps myself and my brother, but he would be wearing a tie. And he also insisted on the boys always putting on a jacket. [LAUGHS] This was always a battle between us. My mother was far more advanced, something of a Bohemian, but you know, much more realistic and pragmatic about these things. And while, of course, she didn't particularly like to see her son away for some period or so, I dare say she had some sort of an idea of what it was all about. And so there was a twinkle in her eye almost as if to say, go in peace and do what you think you ought to be doing. Indeed, that gave me a lot of encouragement. My father wouldn't speak to me [LAUGHS] for two or three weeks before I finally said, this is it. Goodbye, see you in about six months' time. I was sent to France. I was one of a member of a human chain that dealt with illegal immigration of survivors of concentration camps, from displaced persons camps as they were then called, not concentration camps anymore of course, who wanted to go to Palestine. The world was not interested in the remnants of concentration camps, those who had survived by guile, by luck, by anything else. And the only opening that those people had, the only hope these people had was Palestine. But Palestine was closed, as you know, because it was a British mandate, and the Brits had some responsibility towards the Arabs as well with regards to keeping immigration as-- as low in numbers as possible. And so the only way to get into there was to get into there illegally. The Haganah, the forerunner of the Israel Defense Forces, was highly organized and very sophisticated with illegal immigration ships and so on and so forth. But one had to get people down to the Mediterranean ports in France and in Italy from central Germany. And the only way one could get them down was to march them down, literally. They went on foot. And we, or my function together with many others, was to take responsibility of a certain sector within which, at nighttime when it was dark, we would be responsible to guide the group of 30 or 40 refugees and guide them along certain routes until in fact I handed over to a colleague of mine again. I in turn received them from a colleague of mine who was just one step further north, or further-- further east in geographical terms. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us exactly where this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ja, the-- the exact location I can't tell you because I don't remember it. It's not a secret, but I just don't remember it. But it straddled the Franco-Belgian border. And so I was a very rich man. I was supplied with a lot of money, which was going to be used ostensively to bribe the guards if we got into any particular difficulties.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of currency was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was French and Belgian currency depending very much on [LAUGHS] who, in fact, was going to sort of step into our way. During approximately 8 to 10 weeks on this operation--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was during what period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was in the winter of 1947-48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the whole operation took care of something like 3/4 of a million people. It was-- it was a-- a mammoth task. And I handled or I dealt with perhaps something like 2 and 1/2 to 3,000 people during this period there. Of course, we couldn't live in a hotel. The whole thing was totally illegal. I managed to find some quarters illegally in some farmyard in some barn that was somewhere in, in, in Belgium, some way away from the farmhouse. And I stayed--

speaker: interviewer
text: With permission of the farmer, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. I dare say he knew that I was there. I've got no idea. There was snow on the ground, so he must have seen, you know, footprints and all that sort of thing. I was never interfered with. I lived-- I stole potatoes because he had potatoes in, in, in, in clamps like we do here in the UK as well during the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I lived on potatoes and snow because there was basically nothing else to live on. And-- and during the day I, I slept or tried to sleep. And during the night I become a night prowler. And I was active in-- in guiding these people down various side roads and tracks in the woods and so on until in fact we met up again with a colleague of mine who then took over and took them further southwards. So you operated on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ja, but within this framework. Yes, each one was entirely on his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a commander?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. There was somebody fully in charge who I met in Paris when I first arrived there. And I was given basic instructions, but his name, obviously, was not known to me. My name, perhaps, was known to him. I don't know. The names of my colleagues on either side-- the one up front and the one at, at, at, at the end of my run-- I knew only by face. Certainly, I didn't know their names. They didn't know my name. And the whole idea was that if we were to be caught and if we were to be tried or held responsible for this sort of thing, we would not be in a position to give away any-- any other information.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get down from Paris to the Belgian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- it is a very good question. I think we went by train, and we were met somewhere near the-- near, near, near, near a station. But I-- I-- I think it was a lorry or something. But we were dispersed and dropped off in that sort of fashion. And it was a-- it was a clandestine operation. It was totally against the interests of HM government. But I think in order to be fair, there was a lot of collusion and connivance by the Belgians and the French who saw this. They literally saw this-- the Brits never saw these people because they were on the island-- who saw this as a humanitarian operation. And they took a fairly strong anti-British stance. And it's because of that that the whole operation-- at least I think so-- it was to a large extent because of that attitude that the operation was highly successful from our point of view.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your attitude towards this operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- felt fairly proud to be able to help some people. I was-- if you looked at these sort of people, some carried babies on their back. Some were elderly people, but many of them died en route. Fortunately, I-- I didn't have any such experiences at all. They carried a similar sort of what we call plastic bags today, and that, that, that, that contained their worldly belongings. There were people dressed in very pathetic sort of situations. And one of our problems-- well, my problem-- was that just as we were crossing the border, the babies would start crying, and that would possibly give our-- our position away. There were patrols there by fully-qualified frontier guards, both Belgian and French. They had dogs with them, sniffer dogs and so on and so forth. And occasionally, of course, we made contact. And then we would either be waved on, where they would smile at me because I exposed myself, of course, as the leader. These people were entirely relying on me for their safety, and so they should. And that was-- that's what I was there for. I mean, nobody was going to shoot them, but they could have said, so far. No further, and get the hell back again. On two occasions I had to use money, yes. And exactly how much I paid out, the worth, I, I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you carry the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had-- there's a certain, certain, a sort of-- you know, the military had in those days a lot of pouches, ammunition pouches and so on. And there was a fantastic surplus of that sort of stuff. And so I had the webbing of these sort of pouches. And the money, it was, it was notes, paper money because, you know, coins are obviously worth a lower value. And so the weight wasn't all that terrible. And I would say in retrospect that although the guards must have known this because I, I was there for that length of time. And clearly before me there was somebody there. And clearly after me, there was somebody there. And they must have known what was going on. But in, in retrospect, these guards never took advantage of this thing. I mean, they held the upper hand. They could've swooped on us. They could have said, you know, we want 10,000 francs. Or we want, you know, so much money or whatever it may be. I was never exploited on that basis. At times I found it advisable and we could've played by ear, to offer a guy, if he appeared to be somewhat obstreperous, to offer him some money. They took this happily, and this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these men and women, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes, yes. These were the survivors of concentration camps who had to some extent of course been fed again properly in displaced person camps. The displaced persons camps were very similar to the internment camp in which my father was. In other words, they were encircled, and they were under military guard, but they were treated as human beings. And they were obviously-- I've never been in a DP camp, but there obviously must have been quite a lot of hospital work going on there as well because many of these people who survived concentration camps were obviously in a very, very poor state, to say the least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hold conversations with them or get to know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I did. Conversations were always difficult. I found that in Israel later on as well. Although I spoke English and, and German and, and, and some school O-level French, which I, I found very useful on that, on that occasion there in France and Belgium. No, we spoke very little. Most of those people spoke Yiddish or spoke Russian or spoke Polish or-- most of them were survivors of Eastern European Jewry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did this operation come to an end for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me it came to an end in so far that I was being relieved of the thing, and I was then sent onto a specific training course early on in the Alps where the intention was to have groups from different countries, no more than 10, to be trained in small arms combat so that when we went back to our respective countries, we could then train people here to go out to Palestine, as it then still was, and become active as fighters. Such things as how to kill somebody with a knife effectively, how to defend yourself against stick fighting, how to kill people with pistols, how to kill people in unarmed combat. Arms, as such-- rifles, machine guns and so on-- were, of course, taboo, were not allowed in Palestine. And so one had to use other means of-- of-- of-- of warfare as well. We didn't have rifles or bayonets, but we certainly had knives. And we had grenades-- how to use grenades and how to in fact effectively eliminate an enemy position or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the winter of '47, '48, the last winter before the establishment of the state of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And that's the end of tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's very quick. [LAUGHS]
